Most of the chemical elements in our bodies exist in the form of compounds which can be further classified as *inorganic compounds* (lacking carbon) or *organic compounds* (containing carbon). Some of the most important organic compounds that are relevant to sport and exercise metabolism include carbohydrates, fats and proteins. Collectively, these compounds are called the *macronutrients*, and it is through the action of specific biochemical processes (i.e. chemical reactions) that our bodies utilize these food sources to provide our muscles with the energy to exercise. Figure 3.3 shows the general flow of chemical organization from atom to macronutrient.

## Definition

Compounds are substances formed from chemical elements. They are classified into two broad categories: *inorganic compounds*, which lack carbon, and *organic compounds*, which contain carbon. The macronutrients—carbohydrates, fats, and proteins—are the organic compounds most relevant to sport and exercise metabolism.

## Core Principles

- Chemical elements in the body predominantly exist bound together as compounds rather than as free atoms.
- The presence or absence of carbon is the defining criterion separating organic from inorganic compounds.
- Macronutrients (carbohydrates, fats, proteins) serve as the body's primary fuel sources, supplying energy to muscles via biochemical reactions.
- Chemical organization proceeds hierarchically from atoms → compounds → macronutrients (Figure 3.3).

## Sub-concepts

- Inorganic compounds (lacking carbon)
- Organic compounds (containing carbon)
- Macronutrients
    - Carbohydrates
    - Fats
    - Proteins
- Biochemical processes / chemical reactions that mobilize macronutrients for energy

## Field-level Controversies

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

## Practical Applications

- Understanding the organic/inorganic distinction underpins how dietary intake of carbohydrates, fats, and proteins is metabolized to power muscular work during exercise.
- The framework links nutrition (macronutrient consumption) to exercise performance through the biochemical reactions that convert food sources into usable energy for muscle contraction.

- Chemical bonding
- Carbohydrate, fat, and protein metabolism
- Bioenergetics of exercise
